plainMeaning: This peak of Mount Raivata (Girnar) — the very best place in all the worlds and honoured by everyone — is where Lord Nemi reached liberation.
recitation:
taṁ revai-giri-siharaṁ, tiloya-sāraṁ tiloya-jaṇa-mahiyaṁ |
ṭhāṇo tiloya-tilao, tiloya-pahu nemi jahiṁ patto ||

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit demonstrative from Sanskrit tad; neuter accusative/nominative singular taṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Points back to the Raivata-tīrtha just named in the preceding verse, carrying the previous gāthā's praise forward.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit revaa/revai from Sanskrit raivata(ka); the ancient name of the Girnar massif near Junāgaḍh.
wordByWord.notes: First member of the compound revai-giri-siharaṁ; Raivataka = Ujjayanta = Girnar.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit/Prakrit giri, 'mountain, hill'; unchanged in Prakrit.
Prakrit sihara from Sanskrit śikhara, 'crest, summit'; neuter accusative singular siharaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Head of the compound; the object praised throughout the verse.

wordByWord.gloss: the quintessence of the three worlds
wordByWord.etymology: tiloya (Skt. triloka, 'three worlds') + sāra (Skt. sāra, 'essence, best part'); karmadhāraya, neuter accusative siharaṁ-agreeing.
wordByWord.notes: First of four anaphoric tiloya- words; declares the peak the finest thing in the cosmos.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit tiloya from Sanskrit triloka (tri 'three' + loka 'world'): the upper, middle and lower realms.
wordByWord.notes: First member of tiloya-jaṇa-mahiyaṁ; the second tiloya of the verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jaṇa from Sanskrit jana, 'people'; here the inhabitants of all three worlds.
wordByWord.notes: Agent within the compound — the beings who do the worshipping.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit mahiya from Sanskrit mahita (√mah, 'to honour/magnify'), past passive participle; neuter accusative mahiyaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Completes 'honoured by the folk of the three worlds'; agrees with siharaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ṭhāṇa from Sanskrit sthāna, 'place, station, abode' (√sthā, 'to stand'); masculine nominative ṭhāṇo.
wordByWord.notes: Doubles in sense: the 'station/abode' that is, and that 'stands as', the tilaka of the worlds. Predicated of the peak.

wordByWord.etymology: As above, Prakrit tiloya = Sanskrit triloka.
wordByWord.notes: Third tiloya; first member of tiloya-tilao.

wordByWord.gloss: tilaka — the crowning forehead-mark / foremost ornament
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit tilao from Sanskrit tilaka, the auspicious mark worn on the brow; figuratively 'the crest, the most distinguished one'.
wordByWord.notes: Metaphor: the peak adorns the three worlds as a tilaka adorns the forehead — their highest, most auspicious point.

wordByWord.etymology: As above, Prakrit tiloya = Sanskrit triloka.
wordByWord.notes: Fourth and final tiloya; first member of tiloya-pahu (epithet of Nemi).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pahu from Sanskrit prabhu, 'lord, sovereign'.
wordByWord.notes: Forms tiloya-pahu, 'Lord of the three worlds', an epithet of Nemi.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit/Sanskrit nemi, '(felly of a) wheel-rim'; the 22nd Tīrthaṅkara, also Ariṣṭanemi.
wordByWord.notes: Subject of patto; the Jina whose liberation sanctifies the peak.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit relative adverb of place jahiṁ from Sanskrit yatra/yasmin, 'where, in which'.
wordByWord.notes: Relative clause locating Nemi's attainment at this very peak; correlates with 'taṁ' opening the verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit patto from Sanskrit prāpta (√āp with pra-), past passive participle, 'attained'; masculine nominative agreeing with Nemi.
wordByWord.notes: Object 'liberation' is understood — picking up the previous verse's nivvāṇa; here especially Nemi's nirvāṇa on Raivata.

literalTranslation: That peak of Mount Raivata — essence of the three worlds, worshipped by the folk of the three worlds; the abode that stands as the tilaka of the three worlds, where the Lord of the three worlds, Nemi, attained [liberation].
idiomaticTranslation: Behold that summit of Raivata-giri — the very essence of all three worlds, revered by every being in them; it shines as the crowning tilaka upon the brow of the universe, the place where Nemi, Lord of the three worlds, won his liberation.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The poet hammers a single word — tiloya (triloka) — four times, and the repetition is the whole craft of the verse: the Raivata peak is the essence of the three worlds (tiloya-sāra), is worshipped by the people of the three worlds (tiloya-jaṇa-mahiya), stands as the tilaka of the three worlds (tiloya-tilao), and is hallowed by the Lord of the three worlds (tiloya-pahu nemi). What in the preceding verse was praised as the 'three-fold tīrtha' (Nemi's renunciation, omniscience and liberation all at Ujjayanta) is here gathered into one luminous image. The governing metaphor is the tilaka: just as the auspicious mark sits at the highest, most honoured point of the forehead, this summit sits at the cosmos's brow — its most exalted and beautiful spot. The frame 'taṁ … jahiṁ patto' ('that [peak] … where he attained') turns geography into theology: a place is not holy in itself but becomes the 'essence of the worlds' precisely because a Jina reached perfection there. Note that 'ṭhāṇo' (sthāna) carries both senses at once — the peak is the abode of liberation and the thing that 'stands' as the world's ornament. The verb 'patto' is left without an explicit object; the reciter supplies 'nivvāṇa' from the previous gāthā, so the two verses lock together as one breath of praise before the following verse develops the helmsman-and-ocean imagery of Nemi as rescuer.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the Jain principle that a kalyāṇaka-bhūmi — ground touched by a Tīrthaṅkara's auspicious life-events — becomes a tīrtha, a crossing-place worthy of veneration. Nemi (Ariṣṭanemi), the 22nd Jina, is traditionally held to have renounced, gained kevala-jñāna and attained nirvāṇa on this mountain, which is why a mere rock summit can be called the 'essence of the three worlds'. Worship here is not relic-cult but reverence for the site of a soul's final freedom; 'tiloya-pahu' affirms a liberated Jina's sovereignty over the entire triloka cosmology (upper, middle, lower worlds), even as the body is gone.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this gāthā, the worshipper performs an instant mental pilgrimage to Girnar without leaving the prayer-hall: dwelling on the fourfold 'tiloya' refrain trains the mind to see Nemi's victory as cosmic, not local, and to honour every ground a Jina has sanctified.

crossReferences.text: Logassa (Caturviṁśati-stava) — salutation to 'Arittha-nemi' among the 24 Jinas
crossReferences.source: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā v.46 — 'Ujjayanta… tiguṇam iha tittham' (the three-fold tīrtha of Nemi's renunciation, omniscience and liberation)

sources.title: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit (પંચ પ્રતિક્રમણ સૂત્ર – વિધિ સહિત), Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim source text of the verse and its place in the Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā

notes: The four-fold repetition of 'tiloya' (triloka) is deliberate anaphora, preserved in the rendering. 'patto' has no explicit object; 'liberation/nirvāṇa' is carried over from v.46 ('nivvāṇo'). 'ṭhāṇo' (sthāna) is read with a double sense — 'abode' and 'standing/established as' — both legitimate; some editions may construe it purely as 'abode'. 'revai' is taken as Raivata(ka), the standard Prakrit name for Girnar/Ujjayanta; spelling follows the book verbatim and is not normalised. Identification of Nemi's three kalyāṇakas at this peak follows tradition (see v.46) rather than this verse's explicit wording.
